1: A shoot will come up from the stump of Jesse, and a branch from his roots will bear fruit. The Hebrew choter (shoot) and netzer (branch) both denote small, unexpected new growth from what appears dead. Jesse (not David) is named — going back one generation to the pre-dynastic root, suggesting the Davidic dynasty will be cut to ground level and a new beginning made. Matt 2:23 cites netzer as a wordplay on Jesus' hometown of Nazareth (Nazarenos).
2: The Spirit of the LORD will rest on him. Ruach is flagged — the Spirit of wisdom, understanding, counsel, might, knowledge, and fear of the LORD. The sevenfold Spirit-endowment in the LXX became important for later theology (Rev 1:4's seven spirits). The MKT renders ruach as Spirit throughout, capitalizing it to indicate the divine Spirit. The verb rest (nuach) suggests settled, permanent resting — not a temporary charismatic endowment but an abiding presence (cf. John 1:32-33).
3: He will take delight in the fear of the LORD. Shaphat (judge) is flagged. The Messiah will not judge by appearances or rumors — by what he sees or hears — but by righteous judgment. YHWH is flagged as well. This inward, Spirit-driven judgment rather than surface-level assessment anticipates Jesus' refusal of external religious performance as the measure of righteousness (Matt 5:20; John 7:24).
4: With righteousness he will judge the poor and with fairness he will decide for the afflicted. Ruach is flagged — the breath of his lips will strike the wicked. The word (dabar) as weapon — spoken righteousness that executes judgment — is developed in NT as the sword from Christ's mouth (Rev 19:15) and the word of God sharper than any two-edged sword (Heb 4:12). Shaphat here carries forensic weight: legal decision for the poor as the beneficiaries of divine justice.
5: Righteousness will be the belt around his waist, and faithfulness the girdle around his loins. Clothing imagery for character — what girds the Messiah defines his identity. The MKT's belt and girdle distinguish the two parallel terms: tsedaqah (righteousness/justice) and emunah (faithfulness/steadiness). Paul's armor of God (Eph 6:14) draws on this: the belt of truth around the waist, developing the Isaianic image into the Christian life of warfare.
6: The wolf will live peacefully with the lamb. The Hebrew gar (sojourn as a guest/resident alien) — the wolf sojourns alongside the lamb as a guest-resident, not as a predator. The MKT's live peacefully captures the new relational status without importing full domestication. The reversal of predator-prey relationships is the eschatological sign of the age of the Spirit — creation renewed so that its enmities are healed.
7: The cow and the bear will graze together; their calves will lie down side by side; and the lion will eat straw like the ox. The dietary habits of the predators change — lion eating straw is the most radical transformation: the apex predator's nature is altered, not just its behavior. This is not merely peaceful coexistence but a change of what the creature fundamentally is — an image of thorough new-creation transformation.
8: The nursing infant will play at the viper's burrow. The most vulnerable human (infant) playing at the most dangerous animal (venomous serpent) without harm. The MKT's nursing infant and toddler make the human vulnerability concrete. The serpent-at-play image evokes the reversal of Eden (Gen 3:15): the enmity between the seed of the woman and the serpent is ended, but by the defeat of the serpent rather than a truce with it.
9: Nothing will harm or destroy anywhere on my holy mountain, for the earth will be filled with the knowledge of the LORD as the waters cover the sea. Two parallel images for comprehensive new-creation peace: (1) no harm anywhere on the holy mountain — the absence of violence; (2) universal knowledge of the LORD filling the earth as completely as water fills the ocean floor. YHWH is flagged — the divine name is the content of this universal knowledge. Hab 2:14 parallels this verse nearly exactly.
10: In that day the Root of Jesse will stand as a banner for the peoples; the nations will seek him, and his resting place will be glorious. Kavod (glory) is flagged — his resting-place (menuchah) will be glory. The Root of Jesse (not branch from Jesse's root, but Root — identifying him with Jesse's origin, not just his descendant) standing as a banner for the peoples (plural — Gentile nations) anticipates the global mission. Paul cites this in Rom 15:12 for Gentile inclusion in the covenant.
11: The Lord will extend his hand a second time to reclaim the remnant of his people. The second-time reclamation frames the new Exodus: as the first exodus brought Israel out of Egypt, the second brings back scattered exiles. The list of nations (Assyria, Egypt, Pathros, Cush, Elam, Shinar, Hamath, coastlands) suggests the entire known world will be the diaspora from which Israel is gathered. YHWH is flagged — the divine name as the agent of this second gathering.
12: He will raise a banner for the nations and gather the exiled of Israel. The banner (nes) of v. 10 is now raised by the Root of Jesse — the Messiah as the gathering-point for both Israel and the nations. The gathering imagery (gather, assemble) is the language of eschatological pilgrimage. In the NT, the cross becomes the banner that draws all people to Christ (John 12:32): lifted up, he draws all people to himself.
13: The tribal jealousy between Ephraim and Judah — the two rival kingdoms — will end. The cutting-off of Judah's antagonizers and the joint military action against surrounding peoples (Philistines, Edomites, Moabites, Ammonites) envisions national reunification. For NT readers this is spiritualized: the enmity between Jew and Gentile is the deeper division resolved in Christ (Eph 2:14-16).
14: The military imagery — swooping on the Philistines, plundering to the east — can be read as literally nationalistic or typologically as the spread of the gospel overcoming its adversaries. The MKT renders the geographical details accurately; the commentary note is that the NT spiritualizes the military conquest of Canaan into the spiritual warfare of 2 Cor 10:4-5.
15: The LORD will dry up the gulf of the Egyptian Sea. YHWH is flagged — the divine name as the one who dries the sea and waves his hand over the Euphrates to divide it into streams for walking through in sandals. The new Exodus typology is explicit: as Moses divided the sea (Exod 14), the LORD will divide both the Nile delta and the Euphrates to bring the scattered people home. The ruach (breath/spirit/wind) is flagged — the same breath/wind that divided the Red Sea (Exod 14:21) will work the new Exodus.
16: There will be a highway from Assyria for the remnant of his people who survive, just as there was for Israel when they came up from Egypt. The explicit first-Exodus / second-Exodus comparison closes the chapter. The highway through the wilderness (cf. Isa 40:3-5) is the path of the new-covenant community. John the Baptist cites this highway imagery (Luke 3:4-6 citing Isa 40:3-5) as preparation for Jesus' coming. The MKT's highway preserves the road-building imagery central to Isaiah's eschatological vision.
